perturbation.original_text: Promptly following the end of each Calendar Quarter during which RevMed is responsible for activities under the Research Plan, but in no event later than [***] following the end of such Calendar Quarter, RevMed will provide to Sanofi a detailed expense report in form approved by the JRDC with respect to the RevMed R&D Costs incurred by or on behalf of RevMed during such Calendar Quarter consistent with the previous sentence (including, if requested by Sanofi in writing, copies of receipts or invoices from Third Parties for all RevMed R&D Out-of-Pocket Costs) together with an invoice for the same, provided that[***]. Sanofi will reimburse RevMed in Dollars all undisputed amounts within such expense reports under this Section 4.5 within [***] following receipt of the invoice therefor.
perturbation.changed_text: Promptly following the end of each Calendar Quarter during which RevMed is responsible for activities under the Research Plan, but in no event later than [***] following the end of such Calendar Quarter, RevMed will provide to Sanofi a detailed expense report in form approved by the JRDC with respect to the RevMed R&D Costs incurred by or on behalf of RevMed during such Calendar Quarter consistent with the previous sentence (including, if requested by Sanofi in writing, copies of receipts or invoices from Third Parties for all RevMed R&D Out-of-Pocket Costs) together with an invoice for the same, provided that[***]. Sanofi will reimburse RevMed in Dollars all undisputed amounts within such expense reports under this Section 4.5 within 120 days following receipt of the invoice therefor.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has [***] in place of the time frame the payment has to be made, while the modified text specifies 120 days. According to California law, payment should be made within a certain legal timeframe, and this longer period may cause a contradiction if it does not comply with California labor laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 204 mandates that wages earned by employees are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. By establishing a 120-day payment timeline, the company could violate the established California Labor Code guidelines, as the payment is not being given within the required timeframe.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that p
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 120-day payment term, which contradicts California Labor Code § 204, as explained by the LLM Law Explanation. Snippet 1 specifies that wages are due and payable twice during each calendar month, with specific deadlines tied to when the labor was performed. The modified text's 120-day payment term does not comply with these legally mandated timelines for wage payment. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition applies here because the modified contract sets a deadline that potentially violates state law, specifically concerning the timely payment of wages.

perturbation.original_text: All notices which are required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and sufficient if delivered personally, sent by e-mail (and promptly confirmed by personal delivery, registered or certified mail or overnight courier), sent by an internationally-recognized overnight courier or sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, addressed as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices which are required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and sufficient if delivered personally or sent by e-mail, addressed as follows:
perturbation.explanation: The original text describes all notices which are required or permitted that are acceptable, such as delivery in person or via email. The modified text limits these approved methods to only delivery in person or email. This is an issue because the original text is in place to comply with the New York civil practice law and rules, which states that you may deliver in person, overnight delivery, or registered/certified mail.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 2103(b)
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2103(b) outlines the methods for serving papers in civil actions, which includes personal delivery, overnight delivery, and registered or certified mail. By restricting the acceptable methods of notice to only personal delivery or email, the modified text contradicts the CPLR, potentially invalidating notices served through other legally permissible means.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language restricts acceptable methods of notice to personal delivery or email, while the original text includes overnight delivery and registered/certified mail. According to the perturbation explanation and law explanation, this contradicts NY CPLR § 2103(b), which allows for other methods like overnight delivery and registered/certified mail. The scraped law snippet 1 indicates that the requested entry could not be found; however, the Law Explanation still gives the legal context, thus this implies that the changed text creates a legal contradiction by removing legally permissible methods for serving notices, potentially invalidating notices served through those means. Therefore, a contradiction exists.
